text.title: Stack Mandate Trade-off Frame Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for writing an ADR inside a client-mandated stack without triggering a political defensive reaction.

rule.statement: The ADR MUST open with a "Constraints" section that quotes or cites the client's stack mandate verbatim (e.g., "Per Client Engineering Standard ENG-007, all backend services must run on EKS with Java 21 and Postgres 16"). The mandate is presented as policy, not as an opinion to be argued.
rule.rationale: Naming the mandate upfront defuses the political reading. The client architect reads the ADR knowing the dev accepts the constraint; subsequent trade-off analysis lands as engineering, not as challenge.

rule.statement: Every option in the Options section MUST be entirely realisable within the mandated stack. The Options table MUST NOT contain an entry that requires any technology outside the mandate (no "Option 4: switch to Nomad", no "Option 3: use Cassandra instead of Postgres").
rule.rationale: A single out-of-mandate option in the table reframes the whole ADR as a stack-replacement pitch. Keeping all options inside the mandate keeps the ADR within the client's decision authority.

rule.statement: Trade-off vocabulary MUST use the client's existing lexicon for components (their naming for service tiers, their environment names, their cost-centre labels). The dev MUST NOT introduce new architectural terms unless the client's documentation already uses them.
rule.rationale: Imported vocabulary signals "we know better"; reused vocabulary signals "we are inside your system". The latter wins approvals even when the technical content is identical.

rule.statement: Where a trade-off causes real pain (latency, cost, complexity), the ADR MUST propose at least one COSTED mitigation the client can buy — extra node group, larger RDS instance, vendor support tier — instead of implying the pain proves the mandate is wrong.
rule.rationale: "This stack is bad for X" is a recommendation the client cannot act on. "This stack supports X at cost Y" is a procurement item the client architect can approve. The latter form keeps the ADR usable.

rule.statement: The "Consequences" and "Rejected Options" sections MUST NOT contain phrases that imply the mandated stack is suboptimal in general (e.g., "would be better served by", "this stack is not ideal for"). All language MUST be local to the specific trade-off being decided.
rule.rationale: Phrases that generalise from one trade-off to a whole-stack judgement leak through engagement reviews and read as "the vendor wants to replace our stack". Localising language protects the relationship across many ADRs.

rule.statement: Skip Stack Mandate Trade-off Frame when any Skip-If condition holds. Do NOT produce an artefact; surface the gap to the requester.
rule.rationale: A methodology applied outside its scope produces low-signal artefacts that pollute the artefact store; the decision tree needs a clean terminator.

example.code:
## Constraints Per Client Standard ENG-007: backend services run on EKS (Java 21, Postgres 16). This ADR operates entirely within that mandate.
## Options (all on EKS/Java/Postgres) | Option | Latency p95 | Monthly cost | Mitigation needed | |--------|-------------|--------------|---------------------| | A. Single shared Postgres | 80ms | $1.2k | Connection pooler — $200/mo | | B. Read replica + cache | 35ms | $1.6k | Replica + ElastiCache tier — $400/mo | | C. Partitioned write path | 45ms | $1.4k | Operational runbook + 1 EKS node group — $300/mo |
## Decision Option B — meets the 50ms SLA. Cost delta vs A is $400/mo, which is the costed mitigation the client should approve.
example.note: Mandate quoted upfront. All options inside the stack. Each trade-off has a costed mitigation. No "consider X" language.

example.code: ## Options | Option | Notes | |--------|-------| | A. Postgres | Latency is high; this stack is not ideal for hot-write workloads. | | B. Cassandra | Recommended for write-heavy workloads. | | C. Switch to Aurora Serverless | Would scale better than RDS. |
example.note: Mandate ignored. Two of three options are out-of-stack. Generalising language ("this stack is not ideal"). Reads as a stack-replacement pitch — gets the dev disqualified.
